articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds 'Polluter Pays' Principle, Strengthens Environmental Governance
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark judgment, reinforced the 'Polluter Pays' principle, making industries and entities directly liable for environmental damage.
• The ruling mandates comprehensive remediation plans and financial penalties for violations, moving beyond mere compensation.
• It clarifies the role of state pollution control boards and environmental agencies, urging proactive enforcement and stricter monitoring.
• The judgment stems from a public interest litigation concerning industrial pollution in major river basins.
• Experts believe this will significantly impact environmental compliance and sustainable development practices across sectors.
gsSubTopics: Environmental Pollution and Degradation
Environmental Impact Assessment
Conservation
Judiciary
Government Policies & Interventions
prelimsPoints: The 'Polluter Pays' principle is a key tenet of environmental law.
It originated from Principle 16 of the Rio Declaration on Environment and Development (1992).
Supreme Court of India has previously applied this principle in various cases, including Vellore Citizens' Welfare Forum v. Union of India.
National Green Tribunal (NGT) also frequently applies this principle.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's judgment on industrial accountability and environmental protection in India.
Discuss how strengthened environmental governance can contribute to achieving India's Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
Evaluate the effectiveness of existing environmental laws and institutions in India and suggest reforms in light of this judgment.
Examine the balance between economic development and environmental conservation in the context of the ruling.
articles.imageDescription: Scales of justice balancing environment and industry, with a backdrop of a clean river.
articles.trivia: The 'Polluter Pays' principle aims to make the party responsible for pollution bear the costs of preventing or dealing with it, rather than externalizing those costs onto society.

articles.headline: Government Launches 'BharatNet 2.0' for Universal Digital Connectivity
articles.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved the launch of 'BharatNet 2.0', an ambitious project to provide high-speed broadband connectivity to all Gram Panchayats and rural areas by 2028.
• Building on the initial BharatNet project, this phase emphasizes last-mile connectivity through Public-Private Partnership (PPP) models.
• The project aims to bridge the digital divide, enabling access to e-governance services, digital education, telemedicine, and online financial transactions.
• Significant investment has been earmarked, with a focus on leveraging advanced fiber optic and satellite communication technologies.
• This initiative is crucial for India's vision of a 'Digital India' and inclusive growth.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure (Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.)
Science & Technology- Developments and their Applications and Effects in Everyday Life
Government Policies and Interventions for Development in various sectors
prelimsPoints: BharatNet project aims to provide broadband connectivity to all Gram Panchayats in India.
It is implemented by Bharat Broadband Network Limited (BBNL), a Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV).
The project uses Optical Fibre Cable (OFC) for connectivity.
The initial phase aimed to connect 2.5 lakh Gram Panchayats.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of universal digital connectivity for India's socio-economic development, particularly in rural areas.
Analyze the challenges in implementing large-scale infrastructure projects like BharatNet and suggest measures for effective execution.
Evaluate the potential impact of BharatNet 2.0 on e-governance, digital education, and healthcare services in rural India.
Examine the role of Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs) in accelerating digital infrastructure development.
articles.imageDescription: Fiber optic cables converging towards a digital map of rural India.
articles.trivia: The BharatNet project is one of the world's largest rural broadband connectivity programs.

articles.headline: India-Japan Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Focus on Indo-Pacific Security
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-Japan Strategic Dialogue concluded in New Delhi, reaffirming commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region.
• Discussions focused on enhancing defense cooperation, maritime security, and intelligence sharing amidst evolving regional geopolitical dynamics.
• Both nations emphasized strengthening supply chain resilience and expanding collaboration in critical and emerging technologies.
• The dialogue also covered cooperation in disaster management, climate change, and multilateral forums like the QUAD.
• The foreign ministers reiterated their resolve to counter challenges to rules-based international order.
gsSubTopics: India and its Neighborhood- Relations
Bilateral, Regional and Global Groupings and Agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of Policies and Politics of Developed and Developing Countries on India’s interests, Indian Diaspora
prelimsPoints: India and Japan are members of the QUAD grouping.
Malabar naval exercise involves India, Japan, USA, and Australia.
Japan is a significant investor in India's infrastructure projects, including the Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail.
Both countries share concerns regarding regional security and freedom of navigation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the India-Japan strategic partnership for regional stability and global order, particularly in the Indo-Pacific.
Discuss the key areas of cooperation between India and Japan and how they contribute to their shared strategic interests.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for India-Japan relations in the context of changing geopolitical landscape.
Evaluate the role of QUAD in promoting security and economic prosperity in the Indo-Pacific region.
articles.imageDescription: Indian and Japanese flags side-by-side, with a map of the Indo-Pacific region in the background.
articles.trivia: Japan is the only country to have signed a Civil Nuclear Cooperation Agreement with India after India conducted nuclear tests in 1998.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Repo Rate, Cautions on Inflationary Pressures
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) unanimously decided to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged at 6.5%.
• The decision reflects a cautious stance, prioritizing inflation control amidst global uncertainties and domestic food price volatility.
• RBI Governor highlighted persistent inflationary pressures, particularly from crude oil prices and erratic monsoon patterns impacting food supply.
• The central bank reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the target of 4% while supporting economic growth.
• Analysts expect the RBI to maintain a hawkish stance for the foreseeable future, with rate cuts unlikely in the near term.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to Planning, Mobilization of Resources, Growth, Development and Employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary Policy
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) determines the policy interest rates required to achieve the inflation target.
The repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Inflation target set by the Government of India for RBI is 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
Quantitative easing involves increasing the money supply, while quantitative tightening involves reducing it.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors influencing the RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate and its implications for the Indian economy.
Discuss the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control with economic growth in the current global and domestic scenario.
Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary policy tools in managing inflation and stimulating economic activity in India.
Examine the role of the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) in India's economic governance.
articles.imageDescription: RBI logo with graphs showing economic trends and inflation curves.
articles.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) consists of six members: three from the RBI and three external members nominated by the Government of India.

articles.headline: New Policy Framework for Critical Minerals Extraction Unveiled
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Mines released a new comprehensive policy framework aimed at boosting the domestic exploration and extraction of critical minerals.
• The policy introduces incentives for private sector participation, streamlines licensing processes, and emphasizes sustainable mining practices.
• Critical minerals like Lithium, Cobalt, Nickel, and Rare Earth Elements are vital for India's energy transition, defense, and high-tech manufacturing sectors.
• The framework seeks to reduce India's reliance on imports and secure supply chains for these strategic resources.
• It also includes provisions for technological advancements in mineral processing and environmental safeguards.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure (Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.)
Mineral Resources
Government Policies & Interventions
prelimsPoints: Lithium is a critical component for electric vehicle batteries.
India recently discovered significant lithium reserves in Jammu & Kashmir.
Rare Earth Elements (REEs) are used in various high-tech applications, including electronics and defense.
The Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) Act, 1957, governs mining in India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of critical minerals for India's economic growth and national security.
Discuss the key provisions of the new policy framework for critical minerals extraction and its potential impact on domestic production.
Examine the environmental and social challenges associated with mining critical minerals and how the new policy addresses them.
Evaluate India's efforts to secure its critical mineral supply chains in the context of global geopolitical competition.
articles.imageDescription: Various critical minerals like lithium, cobalt, and rare earth elements displayed, with a graphic highlighting their applications in modern technology.
articles.trivia: India's first-ever lithium discovery in Jammu and Kashmir in 2023 was estimated to be 5.9 million tonnes.

articles.headline: Eminent Punjabi Scholar Dr. Rattan Singh Jaggi Passes Away at 97
articles.explainer:
• Renowned Punjabi scholar and literary critic, Dr. Rattan Singh Jaggi, passed away at the age of 97.
• Dr. Jaggi was a prolific writer, known for his extensive research on Sikh literature, Punjabi classical poetry, and comparative religion.
• His magnum opus, 'Dasam Granth Darshan', and his comprehensive dictionary of Punjabi literature are considered invaluable contributions.
• He was a recipient of numerous awards, including the Sahitya Akademi Award, for his profound impact on Punjabi language and culture.
• His demise marks the end of an era for Punjabi literary criticism and scholarship.
gsSubTopics: Indian Culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
prelimsPoints: Dr. Rattan Singh Jaggi was an eminent scholar of Punjabi literature.
He was particularly known for his work on Sikh literature and the Dasam Granth.
The Sahitya Akademi Award is a literary honor in India.
Dasam Granth is a sacred scripture of Sikhism, attributed to Guru Gobind Singh.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of literary figures like Dr. Rattan Singh Jaggi in preserving and promoting regional languages and cultures in India.
Examine the contributions of Sikh literature to the broader Indian literary tradition and its philosophical underpinnings.
Analyze the role of literary criticism in shaping public understanding and appreciation of cultural heritage.
Comment on the challenges faced by regional language scholarship in contemporary India.
articles.imageDescription: A portrait of Dr. Rattan Singh Jaggi, surrounded by books and ancient manuscripts.
articles.trivia: The Dasam Granth is a complex and extensive compilation of religious texts, some attributed to Guru Gobind Singh, the tenth Sikh Guru.

articles.headline: ISRO Prepares for Next-Gen Navigation Satellite Launch
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced preparations for the launch of its next-generation navigation satellite, NVS-02.
• This advanced satellite will enhance the accuracy and coverage of India's indigenous navigation system, NavIC (Navigation with Indian Constellation).
• NVS-02 incorporates new technologies, including L1 band signals for improved civilian use and a Rubidium atomic clock developed indigenously.
• The launch is scheduled from Satish Dhawan Space Centre, Sriharikota, using the GSLV Mk-II rocket.
• This mission underscores India's growing self-reliance in space technology and its commitment to providing robust navigation services.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in Science & Technology
Indigenization of Technology and Developing New Technology
Space
prelimsPoints: NavIC is India's regional satellite navigation system.
It was formerly known as IRNSS (Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System).
NavIC provides two types of services: Standard Positioning Service (SPS) for civilian users and Restricted Service (RS) for authorized users.
The GSLV Mk-II is a three-stage rocket, capable of launching satellites into Geosynchronous Transfer Orbit (GTO).
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of indigenous satellite navigation systems like NavIC for India's defense, economy, and civilian applications.
Analyze the technological advancements incorporated in the next-generation NVS series satellites and their benefits.
Evaluate India's progress in space technology and its implications for global space exploration and applications.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for ISRO in developing and maintaining advanced satellite constellations.
articles.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of the NVS-02 satellite orbiting Earth, with signals emanating towards India.
articles.trivia: NavIC currently covers India and a region extending up to 1,500 km around the Indian mainland.

articles.headline: Lok Sabha Passes Bill to Amend Forest Conservation Act, Sparks Debate
articles.explainer:
• The Lok Sabha passed a controversial bill proposing amendments to the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980.
• The amendments aim to streamline forest land diversion for strategic national projects, border infrastructure, and certain public utility purposes.
• Critics argue that the bill could dilute existing forest protection laws and lead to increased deforestation and biodiversity loss.
• The government maintains that the amendments are necessary for national security and development, while ensuring environmental safeguards.
• The bill now moves to the Rajya Sabha for further consideration.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, Environmental Pollution and Degradation, Environmental Impact Assessment
Government Policies & Interventions
prelimsPoints: The Forest (Conservation) Act was enacted in 1980.
It restricts the de-reservation of forests or use of forest land for non-forest purposes without prior approval of the Central Government.
The Act aims to check deforestation and promote conservation of forests.
Compensatory Afforestation Fund Management and Planning Authority (CAMPA) manages funds for compensatory afforestation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key provisions of the proposed amendments to the Forest (Conservation) Act and their potential impact on India's forest cover and biodiversity.
Discuss the arguments for and against the amendments, considering both developmental needs and environmental protection.
Evaluate the effectiveness of India's forest conservation policies and suggest measures for balancing development with ecological sustainability.
Examine the role of parliamentary debate in shaping environmental legislation in India.
articles.imageDescription: A split image showing a lush green forest on one side and an infrastructure project (e.g., road, railway) on the other, symbolizing the conflict.
articles.trivia: India's National Forest Policy aims to have at least 33% of the country's total geographical area under forest or tree cover.

articles.headline: India Hosts International Conference on Climate-Resilient Agriculture
articles.explainer:
• India hosted a major international conference focused on developing and implementing climate-resilient agricultural practices.
• Delegates from over 50 countries discussed strategies to adapt farming systems to climate change, ensure food security, and promote sustainable livelihoods.
• Key themes included drought-resistant crops, water-efficient irrigation, agro-forestry, and digital agriculture solutions.
• The conference emphasized the need for international collaboration and knowledge sharing to address global agricultural challenges.
• India showcased its initiatives in climate-smart agriculture, including the National Innovations in Climate Resilient Agriculture (NICRA) program.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, Environmental Pollution and Degradation, Environmental Impact Assessment
Agriculture (Major crops, Cropping patterns, Irrigation, Storage, Transport & Marketing, E-technology)
Food Security
prelimsPoints: Climate-resilient agriculture aims to achieve food security and development in the face of climate change.
NICRA (National Innovations in Climate Resilient Agriculture) is an ICAR project launched in 2011.
FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization) is a specialized agency of the United Nations.
Millet crops are often considered climate-resilient due to their hardiness and low water requirements.
mainsPoints: Discuss the urgent need for climate-resilient agriculture in India, considering its vulnerability to climate change impacts.
Analyze the various strategies and technologies that can be adopted to make Indian agriculture more climate-resilient.
Evaluate the role of international cooperation and knowledge sharing in addressing global food security challenges posed by climate change.
Examine how government policies and programs can support farmers in adopting climate-smart agricultural practices.
articles.imageDescription: Farmers working in a field with drought-resistant crops, surrounded by modern agricultural technology.
articles.trivia: India is the world's second-largest producer of rice and wheat, making its agricultural resilience crucial for global food security.

articles.headline: Ministry of Health Announces Nationwide Campaign for Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs)
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health & Family Welfare launched a comprehensive nationwide campaign focusing on prevention, early detection, and management of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs).
• The campaign targets common NCDs like diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular diseases, and various cancers, which account for a significant burden of disease in India.
• It emphasizes lifestyle modifications, regular health screenings, and access to affordable treatment in both urban and rural areas.
• Public awareness programs, community health worker training, and integration of NCD services into primary healthcare are key components.
• This initiative aligns with India's commitment to achieving the Sustainable Development Goal 3 (Good Health and Well-being).
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to Development and Management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
Government Policies & Interventions for Development in various sectors
prelimsPoints: NCDs are chronic diseases that are not passed from person to person.
Major NCDs include cardiovascular diseases, cancers, chronic respiratory diseases, and diabetes.
SDG 3 aims to ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages.
Ayushman Bharat scheme aims to provide health coverage to vulnerable sections of society.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing burden of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) in India and their socio-economic implications.
Analyze the multi-pronged approach adopted by the Ministry of Health to combat NCDs, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses.
Evaluate the role of preventive healthcare and public awareness campaigns in reducing the prevalence of lifestyle diseases.
Examine how India's healthcare infrastructure can be strengthened to provide comprehensive NCD care, especially in rural areas.
articles.imageDescription: People engaging in healthy activities like yoga and walking, with infographics about NCD prevention.
articles.trivia: Globally, NCDs kill 41 million people each year, equivalent to 74% of all deaths.

articles.headline: Archaeological Survey of India Unearths Ancient Buddhist Monastery Remains in Odisha
articles.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced the discovery of significant remains of an ancient Buddhist monastery complex in Odisha's Jajpur district.
• The excavations revealed stupas, prayer halls, monastic cells, and intricate sculptures dating back to the Gupta and post-Gupta periods (4th-7th century CE).
• This discovery sheds new light on the spread of Buddhism in eastern India and the region's historical links with Buddhist centers like Ratnagiri and Lalitgiri.
• Artifacts recovered include terracotta seals, pottery, and Buddha images, providing valuable insights into the cultural and religious practices of the era.
• The site is expected to become a major archaeological and tourist attraction, bolstering cultural tourism in Odisha.
gsSubTopics: Indian Culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
History of India and Indian National Movement.
prelimsPoints: Odisha was a significant center for the spread of Buddhism in ancient India.
Udayagiri, Lalitgiri, and Ratnagiri are known as the 'Diamond Triangle' of Buddhist sites in Odisha.
The Gupta period is often referred to as the 'Golden Age of India' for its achievements in art, science, and literature.
ASI is responsible for archaeological research and the protection of cultural heritage in India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the discovery of ancient Buddhist monastery remains in Odisha for understanding the history and spread of Buddhism in India.
Analyze the architectural and artistic features of Buddhist monasteries during the Gupta and post-Gupta periods, based on such findings.
Examine the role of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) in preserving and promoting India's rich cultural heritage.
Evaluate the potential of such archaeological discoveries to boost cultural tourism and regional development.
articles.imageDescription: Archaeologists excavating ancient ruins, revealing parts of a stupa and ancient sculptures.
articles.trivia: The 'Diamond Triangle' of Odisha (Udayagiri, Lalitgiri, Ratnagiri) represents one of the most important concentrations of Buddhist remains in India.

articles.headline: India's Exports See Marginal Growth Amid Global Economic Headwinds
articles.explainer:
• India's merchandise exports recorded a marginal year-on-year growth of 2.1% in April 2025, reaching approximately $35 billion.
• The growth comes amidst a challenging global economic environment, marked by geopolitical tensions and slowing demand in key markets.
• Sectors like electronics, pharmaceuticals, and certain agricultural products showed resilience, while traditional exports faced headwinds.
• The Ministry of Commerce reiterated its commitment to diversify export markets and boost domestic manufacturing under the 'Make in India' initiative.
• The trade deficit, however, remains a concern due to sustained import demand, particularly for crude oil and capital goods.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to Planning, Mobilization of Resources, Growth, Development and Employment
Government Budgeting
Balance of Payment
prelimsPoints: Merchandise exports refer to the export of physical goods.
Trade deficit occurs when a country's imports exceed its exports.
The 'Make in India' initiative aims to encourage companies to manufacture in India.
Diversification of export markets helps reduce reliance on a few countries.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to the marginal growth in India's exports amidst global economic headwinds.
Discuss the strategies adopted by the Indian government to boost exports and reduce the trade deficit.
Evaluate the effectiveness of initiatives like 'Make in India' and Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes in enhancing India's manufacturing and export capabilities.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for India to integrate further into global supply chains and expand its export basket.
articles.imageDescription: Container ships at a port, symbolizing international trade, with a graph showing slight upward trend.
articles.trivia: India aims to achieve $1 trillion in merchandise exports and $1 trillion in services exports by 2030.

articles.headline: Border Security Forces Enhance Surveillance Along Western Borders
articles.explainer:
• The Border Security Force (BSF) announced the deployment of advanced surveillance technology and increased personnel along India's western borders.
• This move is aimed at curbing infiltration attempts, cross-border smuggling, and drone activities from across the border.
• New measures include integrated border management systems, night vision devices, and enhanced patrolling.
• The decision follows recent intelligence reports indicating heightened activities by non-state actors.
• BSF officials emphasized strengthening coordination with other security agencies for a robust multi-layered defense.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas
Linkages of organized crime with terrorism
prelimsPoints: The BSF is one of India's Central Armed Police Forces (CAPFs).
It is responsible for guarding India's land borders during peacetime and preventing transnational crime.
Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) aims to improve border security by integrating manpower, sensors, networks, and command and control systems.
Drone threats have emerged as a significant challenge for border security.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving nature of security challenges along India's western borders, including infiltration, smuggling, and drone threats.
Analyze the measures being adopted by the Border Security Force (BSF) to enhance border surveillance and management.
Evaluate the effectiveness of integrated border management systems in addressing complex security challenges.
Examine the need for greater coordination among various security agencies to ensure comprehensive border security.
articles.imageDescription: BSF personnel patrolling a border area with surveillance equipment, a drone flying in the background.
articles.trivia: The BSF was raised in 1965 in the aftermath of the 1965 Indo-Pak War.

articles.headline: Union Cabinet Approves Major Funding Boost for National Research Foundation
articles.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved a substantial increase in funding for the National Research Foundation (NRF), earmarking ₹50,000 crore over five years.
• The NRF aims to seed, grow, and facilitate research and development across universities and colleges in India, fostering a culture of innovation.
• The enhanced funding will support multidisciplinary research, develop research infrastructure, and attract top talent into scientific pursuits.
• The initiative is expected to boost India's global ranking in scientific research output and promote collaboration between academia and industry.
• This move aligns with the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020's focus on strengthening research ecosystems.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- Developments and their Applications and Effects in Everyday Life
Indigenization of Technology and Developing New Technology
Government Policies & Interventions
prelimsPoints: The National Research Foundation (NRF) was proposed under the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
Its primary objective is to fund and coordinate research across various disciplines in India.
NEP 2020 advocates for a multidisciplinary approach to education and research.
India's gross expenditure on R&D has been a subject of policy discussions.
mainsPoints: Discuss the critical need for a robust National Research Foundation (NRF) to catalyze research and innovation in India.
Analyze how enhanced funding and a structured framework can transform India's research ecosystem and boost its global scientific standing.
Evaluate the potential impact of the NRF on fostering multidisciplinary research, academic-industry collaboration, and talent retention.
Examine the challenges in promoting a strong research culture in Indian universities and how the NRF aims to address them.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting a network of universities and research labs connected by lines of funding and collaboration.
articles.trivia: The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 aims to make India a global knowledge superpower.

articles.headline: Government Releases Annual Report on Tiger Census, Highlights Conservation Efforts
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change released the annual report of the latest tiger census, showing a steady increase in India's tiger population.
• The report attributes the growth to sustained conservation efforts, including Project Tiger, habitat protection, and anti-poaching measures.
• It highlighted certain tiger reserves that have shown exemplary performance in tiger population growth and management.
• Challenges remain, such as human-wildlife conflict, habitat fragmentation, and the need for stricter enforcement against poaching.
• The report underscores India's commitment to tiger conservation and biodiversity protection.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, Environmental Pollution and Degradation, Environmental Impact Assessment
Biodiversity
articles.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: India conducts a tiger census every four years.
Project Tiger was launched in 1973.
India is home to over 70% of the world's tiger population.
National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA) is a statutory body under the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change.
mainsPoints: Discuss the success and challenges of Project Tiger and other conservation initiatives in increasing India's tiger population.
Analyze the ecological and economic significance of tiger conservation for India's biodiversity and ecosystem health.
Evaluate the strategies employed to mitigate human-wildlife conflict and ensure the long-term survival of tigers.
Examine the role of community participation and international cooperation in successful wildlife conservation efforts.
articles.imageDescription: A majestic tiger in its natural habitat, with graphics showing population growth on a map of India.
articles.trivia: The first nationwide tiger census in India was conducted in 1972.

articles.headline: G20 Finance Ministers Meeting Addresses Global Debt Vulnerabilities and Climate Finance
articles.explainer:
• Finance Ministers and Central Bank Governors from G20 nations convened to discuss pressing global economic issues, including rising debt vulnerabilities in developing countries.
• India, as a key participant, advocated for a collaborative approach to debt restructuring and increased access to climate finance for vulnerable nations.
• Discussions also focused on strengthening the global financial safety net, reforming multilateral development banks, and addressing inflation.
• The meeting highlighted the need for coordinated policy responses to foster sustainable and inclusive global economic growth.
• Climate finance commitments and mechanisms for green investments were a significant agenda item.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, Regional and Global Groupings and Agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of Policies and Politics of Developed and Developing Countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The G20 is an international forum for the governments and central bank governors from 19 countries and the European Union.
India held the G20 Presidency in 2023.
Climate finance refers to local, national, or transnational financing, which may be drawn from public, private, and alternative sources of financing.
Multilateral Development Banks (MDBs) include institutions like the World Bank and IMF.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges posed by rising global debt vulnerabilities, especially for developing countries, and the G20's role in addressing them.
Analyze India's position and contributions to the G20 discussions on global economic stability and climate finance.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current global financial mechanisms in providing adequate climate finance to vulnerable nations.
Examine the need for reforms in multilateral development banks to better serve the needs of the global south.
articles.imageDescription: G20 logo with flags of member countries, overlaid with graphs representing global economic trends.
articles.trivia: The G20 was formed in 1999 in response to several world economic crises.

articles.headline: New Policy Framework Proposed for Sustainable Urban Housing
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs proposed a new policy framework aimed at promoting sustainable and affordable urban housing across India.
• The framework emphasizes green building standards, climate-resilient construction, and the use of indigenous materials.
• It also seeks to address the growing demand for affordable housing by incentivizing private developers and leveraging public-private partnerships.
• Key features include smart city integration, efficient waste management solutions, and enhanced public transport connectivity for new urban developments.
• The policy aims to align urban growth with environmental sustainability and improve the quality of life in cities.
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies
Government Policies & Interventions for Development in various sectors
articles.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: India is experiencing rapid urbanization.
Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (PMAY) aims to provide 'Housing for All' by 2022 (extended).
Smart Cities Mission aims to promote cities that provide core infrastructure and give a decent quality of life.
Green building concepts focus on reducing environmental impact during construction and operation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges of providing sustainable and affordable urban housing in rapidly urbanizing India.
Analyze the key features of the proposed policy framework for urban housing and its potential to address existing issues.
Evaluate the role of green building standards and climate-resilient construction in achieving sustainable urban development.
Examine how public-private partnerships can be leveraged to meet the growing demand for affordable housing in Indian cities.
articles.imageDescription: Modern, eco-friendly apartment buildings in an urban setting, with green spaces and solar panels.
articles.trivia: By 2050, India's urban population is projected to increase by 416 million people.

highlights: Supreme Court reinforces 'Polluter Pays' principle, mandating industries to bear costs of environmental damage.
Government launches 'BharatNet 2.0' to extend high-speed broadband to all rural areas by 2028.
India and Japan conclude strategic dialogue, emphasizing Indo-Pacific security and defense cooperation.
Eminent Punjabi scholar Dr. Rattan Singh Jaggi passes away at 97, leaving a legacy in Sikh literature.
Union Cabinet approves ₹50,000 crore funding for National Research Foundation to boost R&D in India.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the 'Polluter Pays Principle', consider the following statements:
1. It mandates that the party responsible for pollution should bear the costs of preventing or dealing with it.
2. It was first formally adopted at the Rio Declaration on Environment and Development (1992).
3. In India, the National Green Tribunal (NGT) frequently applies this principle in environmental cases.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The Polluter Pays Principle is a fundamental tenet of environmental law that holds polluters accountable for environmental damage. Statement 2 is correct: It originated from Principle 16 of the Rio Declaration on Environment and Development (1992). Statement 3 is correct: In India, both the Supreme Court and the National Green Tribunal (NGT) have consistently applied this principle in various environmental cases.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about India's BharatNet project is/are correct?
1. Its primary aim is to provide high-speed broadband connectivity to all Gram Panchayats.
2. It is implemented by Bharat Broadband Network Limited (BBNL).
3. It primarily uses satellite communication for last-mile connectivity.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The BharatNet project aims to provide broadband connectivity to all Gram Panchayats. Statement 2 is correct: It is implemented by Bharat Broadband Network Limited (BBNL). Statement 3 is incorrect: While BharatNet 2.0 may leverage satellite communication, the primary technology used for the initial phase and a significant part of the project is Optical Fibre Cable (OFC).

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following pairs of critical minerals and their primary applications:
1. Lithium: Electric Vehicle Batteries
2. Cobalt: Superalloys and Magnets
3. Rare Earth Elements: High-tech Electronics and Defense
Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three pairs are correctly matched. Lithium is essential for electric vehicle batteries. Cobalt is used in superalloys for jet engines and in powerful magnets. Rare Earth Elements (REEs) are crucial components in various high-tech applications, including smartphones, computers, and defense systems.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, consider the following statements:
1. It restricts the de-reservation of forests or use of forest land for non-forest purposes without central government approval.
2. It was enacted primarily to promote compensatory afforestation.
3. The Act applies only to notified forest areas and not to deemed forests.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, primarily regulates the diversion of forest land for non-forest purposes. Statement 2 is incorrect: While compensatory afforestation is an outcome, the primary purpose of the Act was to check deforestation and conserve forests, not just promote compensatory afforestation. Statement 3 is incorrect: The Supreme Court's Godavarman judgment extended the applicability of the Act to 'deemed forests' as well, which are not formally notified but meet the definition of forest.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following is NOT a primary objective of the National Research Foundation (NRF) proposed under the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020?
options: (a) To fund and coordinate research across various disciplines in India.
(b) To develop research infrastructure in universities and colleges.
(c) To attract foreign faculty exclusively to Indian research institutions.
(d) To foster a culture of innovation and multidisciplinary research.
prelimsMocks.answer: (c) To attract foreign faculty exclusively to Indian research institutions.
prelimsMocks.explanation: The primary objectives of the NRF include funding and coordinating research, developing research infrastructure, and fostering innovation and multidisciplinary research. While attracting top talent (which may include foreign faculty) is a broader goal for strengthening research, 'exclusively attracting foreign faculty' is not a stated primary objective, and the NRF's focus is on developing indigenous research capabilities and talent across Indian institutions.

mainsMocks.question: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's reinforced 'Polluter Pays' principle for industrial accountability and environmental protection in India. Discuss the challenges in its effective implementation and suggest measures for greater compliance.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Conservation, Environmental Pollution and Degradation, Environmental Impact Assessment. GS2: Government Policies and Interventions for Development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the 'Polluter Pays' principle and its origin. Discuss the Supreme Court's latest judgment and its significance. Analyze positive implications (e.g., increased accountability, deterrence, resource mobilization for remediation). Identify challenges in implementation (e.g., identifying polluters, calculating damages, enforcement, legal loopholes). Suggest measures for effective implementation (e.g., stricter EIA, robust monitoring, public participation, fast-track environmental courts, technological solutions).

mainsMocks.question: Discuss the strategic importance of critical minerals for India's economic growth and national security. Evaluate the new policy framework aimed at boosting domestic exploration and extraction of these minerals, highlighting its potential benefits and associated challenges.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.; Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life. GS2: Government Policies and Interventions for Development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
mainsMocks.approach: Define critical minerals and explain their strategic importance for sectors like EV, defense, electronics, and renewable energy. Discuss India's current reliance on imports. Detail the key provisions of the new policy framework (incentives, streamlined licensing, sustainable practices). Analyze potential benefits (reduced import dependency, 'Make in India' boost, job creation). Identify challenges (environmental concerns, land acquisition, technological expertise, capital investment, global competition). Conclude with a forward-looking statement on resource security.

mainsMocks.question: The launch of 'BharatNet 2.0' aims to bridge the digital divide in rural India. Examine the socio-economic impact of universal digital connectivity on rural development, e-governance, and human capital. What challenges might this ambitious project face, and how can they be mitigated?
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Infrastructure (Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.); Science and Technology- Developments and their Applications and Effects in Everyday Life. GS2: Issues relating to Development and Management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce BharatNet 2.0 and its objective. Discuss the socio-economic benefits: improved access to education (e-learning), healthcare (telemedicine), financial services (digital payments), e-governance, market access for farmers, and employment generation. Identify challenges: last-mile connectivity, digital literacy, infrastructure maintenance, cybersecurity, affordability, and coordination issues. Suggest mitigation strategies: PPP models, community involvement, skill development, policy support, and robust regulatory framework.

quickBytes.text: The 'Polluter Pays' principle originated from Principle 16 of the Rio Declaration (1992).
BharatNet project is implemented by Bharat Broadband Network Limited (BBNL).
India and Japan are key members of the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (QUAD).
The RBI's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) comprises six members.
Lithium is a critical mineral essential for electric vehicle batteries.
NavIC is India's indigenous regional satellite navigation system.
The Forest (Conservation) Act was enacted in 1980 to check deforestation.
Project Tiger, India's tiger conservation program, was launched in 1973.
The G20 was formed in 1999 in response to global economic crises.
The National Research Foundation (NRF) was proposed under NEP 2020.

GS1.headline: Eminent Punjabi Scholar Dr. Rattan Singh Jaggi Passes Away at 97
GS1.explainer:
• Renowned Punjabi scholar and literary critic, Dr. Rattan Singh Jaggi, passed away at the age of 97.
• Dr. Jaggi was a prolific writer, known for his extensive research on Sikh literature, Punjabi classical poetry, and comparative religion.
• His magnum opus, 'Dasam Granth Darshan', and his comprehensive dictionary of Punjabi literature are considered invaluable contributions.
• He was a recipient of numerous awards, including the Sahitya Akademi Award, for his profound impact on Punjabi language and culture.
• His demise marks the end of an era for Punjabi literary criticism and scholarship.
gsSubTopics: Indian Culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
prelimsPoints: Dr. Rattan Singh Jaggi was an eminent scholar of Punjabi literature.
He was particularly known for his work on Sikh literature and the Dasam Granth.
The Sahitya Akademi Award is a literary honor in India.
Dasam Granth is a sacred scripture of Sikhism, attributed to Guru Gobind Singh.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of literary figures like Dr. Rattan Singh Jaggi in preserving and promoting regional languages and cultures in India.
Examine the contributions of Sikh literature to the broader Indian literary tradition and its philosophical underpinnings.
Analyze the role of literary criticism in shaping public understanding and appreciation of cultural heritage.
Comment on the challenges faced by regional language scholarship in contemporary India.
GS1.imageDescription: A portrait of Dr. Rattan Singh Jaggi, surrounded by books and ancient manuscripts.
GS1.trivia: The Dasam Granth is a complex and extensive compilation of religious texts, some attributed to Guru Gobind Singh, the tenth Sikh Guru.

GS1.headline: Archaeological Survey of India Unearths Ancient Buddhist Monastery Remains in Odisha
GS1.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced the discovery of significant remains of an ancient Buddhist monastery complex in Odisha's Jajpur district.
• The excavations revealed stupas, prayer halls, monastic cells, and intricate sculptures dating back to the Gupta and post-Gupta periods (4th-7th century CE).
• This discovery sheds new light on the spread of Buddhism in eastern India and the region's historical links with Buddhist centers like Ratnagiri and Lalitgiri.
• Artifacts recovered include terracotta seals, pottery, and Buddha images, providing valuable insights into the cultural and religious practices of the era.
• The site is expected to become a major archaeological and tourist attraction, bolstering cultural tourism in Odisha.
gsSubTopics: Indian Culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
History of India and Indian National Movement.
prelimsPoints: Odisha was a significant center for the spread of Buddhism in ancient India.
Udayagiri, Lalitgiri, and Ratnagiri are known as the 'Diamond Triangle' of Buddhist sites in Odisha.
The Gupta period is often referred to as the 'Golden Age of India' for its achievements in art, science, and literature.
ASI is responsible for archaeological research and the protection of cultural heritage in India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the discovery of ancient Buddhist monastery remains in Odisha for understanding the history and spread of Buddhism in India.
Analyze the architectural and artistic features of Buddhist monasteries during the Gupta and post-Gupta periods, based on such findings.
Examine the role of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) in preserving and promoting India's rich cultural heritage.
Evaluate the potential of such archaeological discoveries to boost cultural tourism and regional development.
GS1.imageDescription: Archaeologists excavating ancient ruins, revealing parts of a stupa and ancient sculptures.
GS1.trivia: The 'Diamond Triangle' of Odisha (Udayagiri, Lalitgiri, Ratnagiri) represents one of the most important concentrations of Buddhist remains in India.

GS2.headline: India-Japan Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Focus on Indo-Pacific Security
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-Japan Strategic Dialogue concluded in New Delhi, reaffirming commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region.
• Discussions focused on enhancing defense cooperation, maritime security, and intelligence sharing amidst evolving regional geopolitical dynamics.
• Both nations emphasized strengthening supply chain resilience and expanding collaboration in critical and emerging technologies.
• The dialogue also covered cooperation in disaster management, climate change, and multilateral forums like the QUAD.
• The foreign ministers reiterated their resolve to counter challenges to rules-based international order.
gsSubTopics: India and its Neighborhood- Relations
Bilateral, Regional and Global Groupings and Agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of Policies and Politics of Developed and Developing Countries on India’s interests, Indian Diaspora
prelimsPoints: India and Japan are members of the QUAD grouping.
Malabar naval exercise involves India, Japan, USA, and Australia.
Japan is a significant investor in India's infrastructure projects, including the Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail.
Both countries share concerns regarding regional security and freedom of navigation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the India-Japan strategic partnership for regional stability and global order, particularly in the Indo-Pacific.
Discuss the key areas of cooperation between India and Japan and how they contribute to their shared strategic interests.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for India-Japan relations in the context of changing geopolitical landscape.
Evaluate the role of QUAD in promoting security and economic prosperity in the Indo-Pacific region.
GS2.imageDescription: Indian and Japanese flags side-by-side, with a map of the Indo-Pacific region in the background.
GS2.trivia: Japan is the only country to have signed a Civil Nuclear Cooperation Agreement with India after India conducted nuclear tests in 1998.

GS2.headline: Ministry of Health Announces Nationwide Campaign for Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs)
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health & Family Welfare launched a comprehensive nationwide campaign focusing on prevention, early detection, and management of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs).
• The campaign targets common NCDs like diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular diseases, and various cancers, which account for a significant burden of disease in India.
• It emphasizes lifestyle modifications, regular health screenings, and access to affordable treatment in both urban and rural areas.
• Public awareness programs, community health worker training, and integration of NCD services into primary healthcare are key components.
• This initiative aligns with India's commitment to achieving the Sustainable Development Goal 3 (Good Health and Well-being).
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to Development and Management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
Government Policies & Interventions for Development in various sectors
prelimsPoints: NCDs are chronic diseases that are not passed from person to person.
Major NCDs include cardiovascular diseases, cancers, chronic respiratory diseases, and diabetes.
SDG 3 aims to ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages.
Ayushman Bharat scheme aims to provide health coverage to vulnerable sections of society.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing burden of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) in India and their socio-economic implications.
Analyze the multi-pronged approach adopted by the Ministry of Health to combat NCDs, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses.
Evaluate the role of preventive healthcare and public awareness campaigns in reducing the prevalence of lifestyle diseases.
Examine how India's healthcare infrastructure can be strengthened to provide comprehensive NCD care, especially in rural areas.
GS2.imageDescription: People engaging in healthy activities like yoga and walking, with infographics about NCD prevention.
GS2.trivia: Globally, NCDs kill 41 million people each year, equivalent to 74% of all deaths.

GS2.headline: G20 Finance Ministers Meeting Addresses Global Debt Vulnerabilities and Climate Finance
GS2.explainer:
• Finance Ministers and Central Bank Governors from G20 nations convened to discuss pressing global economic issues, including rising debt vulnerabilities in developing countries.
• India, as a key participant, advocated for a collaborative approach to debt restructuring and increased access to climate finance for vulnerable nations.
• Discussions also focused on strengthening the global financial safety net, reforming multilateral development banks, and addressing inflation.
• The meeting highlighted the need for coordinated policy responses to foster sustainable and inclusive global economic growth.
• Climate finance commitments and mechanisms for green investments were a significant agenda item.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, Regional and Global Groupings and Agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of Policies and Politics of Developed and Developing Countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The G20 is an international forum for the governments and central bank governors from 19 countries and the European Union.
India held the G20 Presidency in 2023.
Climate finance refers to local, national, or transnational financing, which may be drawn from public, private, and alternative sources of financing.
Multilateral Development Banks (MDBs) include institutions like the World Bank and IMF.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges posed by rising global debt vulnerabilities, especially for developing countries, and the G20's role in addressing them.
Analyze India's position and contributions to the G20 discussions on global economic stability and climate finance.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current global financial mechanisms in providing adequate climate finance to vulnerable nations.
Examine the need for reforms in multilateral development banks to better serve the needs of the global south.
GS2.imageDescription: G20 logo with flags of member countries, overlaid with graphs representing global economic trends.
GS2.trivia: The G20 was formed in 1999 in response to several world economic crises.

GS2.headline: New Policy Framework Proposed for Sustainable Urban Housing
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs proposed a new policy framework aimed at promoting sustainable and affordable urban housing across India.
• The framework emphasizes green building standards, climate-resilient construction, and the use of indigenous materials.
• It also seeks to address the growing demand for affordable housing by incentivizing private developers and leveraging public-private partnerships.
• Key features include smart city integration, efficient waste management solutions, and enhanced public transport connectivity for new urban developments.
• The policy aims to align urban growth with environmental sustainability and improve the quality of life in cities.
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies
Government Policies & Interventions for Development in various sectors
GS2.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: India is experiencing rapid urbanization.
Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (PMAY) aims to provide 'Housing for All' by 2022 (extended).
Smart Cities Mission aims to promote cities that provide core infrastructure and give a decent quality of life.
Green building concepts focus on reducing environmental impact during construction and operation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges of providing sustainable and affordable urban housing in rapidly urbanizing India.
Analyze the key features of the proposed policy framework for urban housing and its potential to address existing issues.
Evaluate the role of green building standards and climate-resilient construction in achieving sustainable urban development.
Examine how public-private partnerships can be leveraged to meet the growing demand for affordable housing in Indian cities.
GS2.imageDescription: Modern, eco-friendly apartment buildings in an urban setting, with green spaces and solar panels.
GS2.trivia: By 2050, India's urban population is projected to increase by 416 million people.

GS3.headline: Supreme Court Upholds 'Polluter Pays' Principle, Strengthens Environmental Governance
GS3.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark judgment, reinforced the 'Polluter Pays' principle, making industries and entities directly liable for environmental damage.
• The ruling mandates comprehensive remediation plans and financial penalties for violations, moving beyond mere compensation.
• It clarifies the role of state pollution control boards and environmental agencies, urging proactive enforcement and stricter monitoring.
• The judgment stems from a public interest litigation concerning industrial pollution in major river basins.
• Experts believe this will significantly impact environmental compliance and sustainable development practices across sectors.
gsSubTopics: Environmental Pollution and Degradation
Environmental Impact Assessment
Conservation
Judiciary
Government Policies & Interventions
prelimsPoints: The 'Polluter Pays' principle is a key tenet of environmental law.
It originated from Principle 16 of the Rio Declaration on Environment and Development (1992).
Supreme Court of India has previously applied this principle in various cases, including Vellore Citizens' Welfare Forum v. Union of India.
National Green Tribunal (NGT) also frequently applies this principle.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's judgment on industrial accountability and environmental protection in India.
Discuss how strengthened environmental governance can contribute to achieving India's Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
Evaluate the effectiveness of existing environmental laws and institutions in India and suggest reforms in light of this judgment.
Examine the balance between economic development and environmental conservation in the context of the ruling.
GS3.imageDescription: Scales of justice balancing environment and industry, with a backdrop of a clean river.
GS3.trivia: The 'Polluter Pays' principle aims to make the party responsible for pollution bear the costs of preventing or dealing with it, rather than externalizing those costs onto society.

GS3.headline: Government Launches 'BharatNet 2.0' for Universal Digital Connectivity
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved the launch of 'BharatNet 2.0', an ambitious project to provide high-speed broadband connectivity to all Gram Panchayats and rural areas by 2028.
• Building on the initial BharatNet project, this phase emphasizes last-mile connectivity through Public-Private Partnership (PPP) models.
• The project aims to bridge the digital divide, enabling access to e-governance services, digital education, telemedicine, and online financial transactions.
• Significant investment has been earmarked, with a focus on leveraging advanced fiber optic and satellite communication technologies.
• This initiative is crucial for India's vision of a 'Digital India' and inclusive growth.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure (Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.)
Science & Technology- Developments and their Applications and Effects in Everyday Life
Government Policies and Interventions for Development in various sectors
prelimsPoints: BharatNet project aims to provide broadband connectivity to all Gram Panchayats in India.
It is implemented by Bharat Broadband Network Limited (BBNL), a Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV).
The project uses Optical Fibre Cable (OFC) for connectivity.
The initial phase aimed to connect 2.5 lakh Gram Panchayats.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of universal digital connectivity for India's socio-economic development, particularly in rural areas.
Analyze the challenges in implementing large-scale infrastructure projects like BharatNet and suggest measures for effective execution.
Evaluate the potential impact of BharatNet 2.0 on e-governance, digital education, and healthcare services in rural India.
Examine the role of Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs) in accelerating digital infrastructure development.
GS3.imageDescription: Fiber optic cables converging towards a digital map of rural India.
GS3.trivia: The BharatNet project is one of the world's largest rural broadband connectivity programs.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Repo Rate, Cautions on Inflationary Pressures
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) unanimously decided to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged at 6.5%.
• The decision reflects a cautious stance, prioritizing inflation control amidst global uncertainties and domestic food price volatility.
• RBI Governor highlighted persistent inflationary pressures, particularly from crude oil prices and erratic monsoon patterns impacting food supply.
• The central bank reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the target of 4% while supporting economic growth.
• Analysts expect the RBI to maintain a hawkish stance for the foreseeable future, with rate cuts unlikely in the near term.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to Planning, Mobilization of Resources, Growth, Development and Employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary Policy
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) determines the policy interest rates required to achieve the inflation target.
The repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Inflation target set by the Government of India for RBI is 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
Quantitative easing involves increasing the money supply, while quantitative tightening involves reducing it.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors influencing the RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate and its implications for the Indian economy.
Discuss the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control with economic growth in the current global and domestic scenario.
Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary policy tools in managing inflation and stimulating economic activity in India.
Examine the role of the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) in India's economic governance.
GS3.imageDescription: RBI logo with graphs showing economic trends and inflation curves.
GS3.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) consists of six members: three from the RBI and three external members nominated by the Government of India.

GS3.headline: New Policy Framework for Critical Minerals Extraction Unveiled
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Mines released a new comprehensive policy framework aimed at boosting the domestic exploration and extraction of critical minerals.
• The policy introduces incentives for private sector participation, streamlines licensing processes, and emphasizes sustainable mining practices.
• Critical minerals like Lithium, Cobalt, Nickel, and Rare Earth Elements are vital for India's energy transition, defense, and high-tech manufacturing sectors.
• The framework seeks to reduce India's reliance on imports and secure supply chains for these strategic resources.
• It also includes provisions for technological advancements in mineral processing and environmental safeguards.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure (Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.)
Mineral Resources
Government Policies & Interventions
prelimsPoints: Lithium is a critical component for electric vehicle batteries.
India recently discovered significant lithium reserves in Jammu & Kashmir.
Rare Earth Elements (REEs) are used in various high-tech applications, including electronics and defense.
The Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) Act, 1957, governs mining in India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of critical minerals for India's economic growth and national security.
Discuss the key provisions of the new policy framework for critical minerals extraction and its potential impact on domestic production.
Examine the environmental and social challenges associated with mining critical minerals and how the new policy addresses them.
Evaluate India's efforts to secure its critical mineral supply chains in the context of global geopolitical competition.
GS3.imageDescription: Various critical minerals like lithium, cobalt, and rare earth elements displayed, with a graphic highlighting their applications in modern technology.
GS3.trivia: India's first-ever lithium discovery in Jammu and Kashmir in 2023 was estimated to be 5.9 million tonnes.

GS3.headline: ISRO Prepares for Next-Gen Navigation Satellite Launch
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced preparations for the launch of its next-generation navigation satellite, NVS-02.
• This advanced satellite will enhance the accuracy and coverage of India's indigenous navigation system, NavIC (Navigation with Indian Constellation).
• NVS-02 incorporates new technologies, including L1 band signals for improved civilian use and a Rubidium atomic clock developed indigenously.
• The launch is scheduled from Satish Dhawan Space Centre, Sriharikota, using the GSLV Mk-II rocket.
• This mission underscores India's growing self-reliance in space technology and its commitment to providing robust navigation services.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in Science & Technology
Indigenization of Technology and Developing New Technology
Space
prelimsPoints: NavIC is India's regional satellite navigation system.
It was formerly known as IRNSS (Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System).
NavIC provides two types of services: Standard Positioning Service (SPS) for civilian users and Restricted Service (RS) for authorized users.
The GSLV Mk-II is a three-stage rocket, capable of launching satellites into Geosynchronous Transfer Orbit (GTO).
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of indigenous satellite navigation systems like NavIC for India's defense, economy, and civilian applications.
Analyze the technological advancements incorporated in the next-generation NVS series satellites and their benefits.
Evaluate India's progress in space technology and its implications for global space exploration and applications.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for ISRO in developing and maintaining advanced satellite constellations.
GS3.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of the NVS-02 satellite orbiting Earth, with signals emanating towards India.
GS3.trivia: NavIC currently covers India and a region extending up to 1,500 km around the Indian mainland.

GS3.headline: Lok Sabha Passes Bill to Amend Forest Conservation Act, Sparks Debate
GS3.explainer:
• The Lok Sabha passed a controversial bill proposing amendments to the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980.
• The amendments aim to streamline forest land diversion for strategic national projects, border infrastructure, and certain public utility purposes.
• Critics argue that the bill could dilute existing forest protection laws and lead to increased deforestation and biodiversity loss.
• The government maintains that the amendments are necessary for national security and development, while ensuring environmental safeguards.
• The bill now moves to the Rajya Sabha for further consideration.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, Environmental Pollution and Degradation, Environmental Impact Assessment
Government Policies & Interventions
prelimsPoints: The Forest (Conservation) Act was enacted in 1980.
It restricts the de-reservation of forests or use of forest land for non-forest purposes without prior approval of the Central Government.
The Act aims to check deforestation and promote conservation of forests.
Compensatory Afforestation Fund Management and Planning Authority (CAMPA) manages funds for compensatory afforestation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key provisions of the proposed amendments to the Forest (Conservation) Act and their potential impact on India's forest cover and biodiversity.
Discuss the arguments for and against the amendments, considering both developmental needs and environmental protection.
Evaluate the effectiveness of India's forest conservation policies and suggest measures for balancing development with ecological sustainability.
Examine the role of parliamentary debate in shaping environmental legislation in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A split image showing a lush green forest on one side and an infrastructure project (e.g., road, railway) on the other, symbolizing the conflict.
GS3.trivia: India's National Forest Policy aims to have at least 33% of the country's total geographical area under forest or tree cover.

GS3.headline: India Hosts International Conference on Climate-Resilient Agriculture
GS3.explainer:
• India hosted a major international conference focused on developing and implementing climate-resilient agricultural practices.
• Delegates from over 50 countries discussed strategies to adapt farming systems to climate change, ensure food security, and promote sustainable livelihoods.
• Key themes included drought-resistant crops, water-efficient irrigation, agro-forestry, and digital agriculture solutions.
• The conference emphasized the need for international collaboration and knowledge sharing to address global agricultural challenges.
• India showcased its initiatives in climate-smart agriculture, including the National Innovations in Climate Resilient Agriculture (NICRA) program.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, Environmental Pollution and Degradation, Environmental Impact Assessment
Agriculture (Major crops, Cropping patterns, Irrigation, Storage, Transport & Marketing, E-technology)
Food Security
prelimsPoints: Climate-resilient agriculture aims to achieve food security and development in the face of climate change.
NICRA (National Innovations in Climate Resilient Agriculture) is an ICAR project launched in 2011.
FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization) is a specialized agency of the United Nations.
Millet crops are often considered climate-resilient due to their hardiness and low water requirements.
mainsPoints: Discuss the urgent need for climate-resilient agriculture in India, considering its vulnerability to climate change impacts.
Analyze the various strategies and technologies that can be adopted to make Indian agriculture more climate-resilient.
Evaluate the role of international cooperation and knowledge sharing in addressing global food security challenges posed by climate change.
Examine how government policies and programs can support farmers in adopting climate-smart agricultural practices.
GS3.imageDescription: Farmers working in a field with drought-resistant crops, surrounded by modern agricultural technology.
GS3.trivia: India is the world's second-largest producer of rice and wheat, making its agricultural resilience crucial for global food security.

GS3.headline: India's Exports See Marginal Growth Amid Global Economic Headwinds
GS3.explainer:
• India's merchandise exports recorded a marginal year-on-year growth of 2.1% in April 2025, reaching approximately $35 billion.
• The growth comes amidst a challenging global economic environment, marked by geopolitical tensions and slowing demand in key markets.
• Sectors like electronics, pharmaceuticals, and certain agricultural products showed resilience, while traditional exports faced headwinds.
• The Ministry of Commerce reiterated its commitment to diversify export markets and boost domestic manufacturing under the 'Make in India' initiative.
• The trade deficit, however, remains a concern due to sustained import demand, particularly for crude oil and capital goods.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to Planning, Mobilization of Resources, Growth, Development and Employment
Government Budgeting
Balance of Payment
prelimsPoints: Merchandise exports refer to the export of physical goods.
Trade deficit occurs when a country's imports exceed its exports.
The 'Make in India' initiative aims to encourage companies to manufacture in India.
Diversification of export markets helps reduce reliance on a few countries.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to the marginal growth in India's exports amidst global economic headwinds.
Discuss the strategies adopted by the Indian government to boost exports and reduce the trade deficit.
Evaluate the effectiveness of initiatives like 'Make in India' and Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes in enhancing India's manufacturing and export capabilities.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for India to integrate further into global supply chains and expand its export basket.
GS3.imageDescription: Container ships at a port, symbolizing international trade, with a graph showing slight upward trend.
GS3.trivia: India aims to achieve $1 trillion in merchandise exports and $1 trillion in services exports by 2030.

GS3.headline: Border Security Forces Enhance Surveillance Along Western Borders
GS3.explainer:
• The Border Security Force (BSF) announced the deployment of advanced surveillance technology and increased personnel along India's western borders.
• This move is aimed at curbing infiltration attempts, cross-border smuggling, and drone activities from across the border.
• New measures include integrated border management systems, night vision devices, and enhanced patrolling.
• The decision follows recent intelligence reports indicating heightened activities by non-state actors.
• BSF officials emphasized strengthening coordination with other security agencies for a robust multi-layered defense.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas
Linkages of organized crime with terrorism
prelimsPoints: The BSF is one of India's Central Armed Police Forces (CAPFs).
It is responsible for guarding India's land borders during peacetime and preventing transnational crime.
Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) aims to improve border security by integrating manpower, sensors, networks, and command and control systems.
Drone threats have emerged as a significant challenge for border security.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving nature of security challenges along India's western borders, including infiltration, smuggling, and drone threats.
Analyze the measures being adopted by the Border Security Force (BSF) to enhance border surveillance and management.
Evaluate the effectiveness of integrated border management systems in addressing complex security challenges.
Examine the need for greater coordination among various security agencies to ensure comprehensive border security.
GS3.imageDescription: BSF personnel patrolling a border area with surveillance equipment, a drone flying in the background.
GS3.trivia: The BSF was raised in 1965 in the aftermath of the 1965 Indo-Pak War.

GS3.headline: Union Cabinet Approves Major Funding Boost for National Research Foundation
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved a substantial increase in funding for the National Research Foundation (NRF), earmarking ₹50,000 crore over five years.
• The NRF aims to seed, grow, and facilitate research and development across universities and colleges in India, fostering a culture of innovation.
• The enhanced funding will support multidisciplinary research, develop research infrastructure, and attract top talent into scientific pursuits.
• The initiative is expected to boost India's global ranking in scientific research output and promote collaboration between academia and industry.
• This move aligns with the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020's focus on strengthening research ecosystems.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- Developments and their Applications and Effects in Everyday Life
Indigenization of Technology and Developing New Technology
Government Policies & Interventions
prelimsPoints: The National Research Foundation (NRF) was proposed under the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
Its primary objective is to fund and coordinate research across various disciplines in India.
NEP 2020 advocates for a multidisciplinary approach to education and research.
India's gross expenditure on R&D has been a subject of policy discussions.
mainsPoints: Discuss the critical need for a robust National Research Foundation (NRF) to catalyze research and innovation in India.
Analyze how enhanced funding and a structured framework can transform India's research ecosystem and boost its global scientific standing.
Evaluate the potential impact of the NRF on fostering multidisciplinary research, academic-industry collaboration, and talent retention.
Examine the challenges in promoting a strong research culture in Indian universities and how the NRF aims to address them.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic depicting a network of universities and research labs connected by lines of funding and collaboration.
GS3.trivia: The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 aims to make India a global knowledge superpower.

GS3.headline: Government Releases Annual Report on Tiger Census, Highlights Conservation Efforts
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change released the annual report of the latest tiger census, showing a steady increase in India's tiger population.
• The report attributes the growth to sustained conservation efforts, including Project Tiger, habitat protection, and anti-poaching measures.
• It highlighted certain tiger reserves that have shown exemplary performance in tiger population growth and management.
• Challenges remain, such as human-wildlife conflict, habitat fragmentation, and the need for stricter enforcement against poaching.
• The report underscores India's commitment to tiger conservation and biodiversity protection.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, Environmental Pollution and Degradation, Environmental Impact Assessment
Biodiversity
GS3.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: India conducts a tiger census every four years.
Project Tiger was launched in 1973.
India is home to over 70% of the world's tiger population.
National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA) is a statutory body under the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change.
mainsPoints: Discuss the success and challenges of Project Tiger and other conservation initiatives in increasing India's tiger population.
Analyze the ecological and economic significance of tiger conservation for India's biodiversity and ecosystem health.
Evaluate the strategies employed to mitigate human-wildlife conflict and ensure the long-term survival of tigers.
Examine the role of community participation and international cooperation in successful wildlife conservation efforts.
GS3.imageDescription: A majestic tiger in its natural habitat, with graphics showing population growth on a map of India.
GS3.trivia: The first nationwide tiger census in India was conducted in 1972.

sources: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
